--- SOURCE 4: Multi-technique operando methods and instruments for ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938

SUMMARY:
_Operando_ (working or operating) methodology is the simultaneous analysis of the catalyst activity and selectivity (function) reaction mixture using any analytical technique and the catalyst (structure) using characterization technique(s).5,6 That is, _operando_ methodology is one step ahead of _in situ_ methodology, which means in place or on site and is applied to both working and non-working conditions while focusing only on the catalyst characterization part. The term _in situ_ is also used for spectra collection of a catalytic sample during treatment at variable temperature, pressure, and reactant concentration but without simultaneous product composition analysis, even when the treatment was performed under reaction conditions.6 Thus, with _operando_ methodology, we can understand the catalyst structure plus kinetics in one experiment.5,6 The data obtained in these systems enable us to assess structure-reactivity relationships more reliably (i.e., to correlate the catalyst structure and active site configuration with a specific catalytic performance).2

FULL CONTENT:
Multi-technique operando methods and instruments for simultaneous assessment of thermal catalysis structure, performance, dynamics, and kinetics - ScienceDirect

Review

Multi-technique _operando_ methods and instruments for simultaneous assessment of thermal catalysis structure, performance, dynamics, and kinetics
==========================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Vijay K.Velisoju 1, Shekhar R.Kulkarni 1, Mengmeng Cui 1, Abdallah I.M.Rabee 2 3, Pasi Paalanen 1, Jabor Rabeah 2, Matteo Maestri 4, [Angelika Brückner 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/7005588484/angelika-bruckner), Javier Ruiz-Martinez 1, Pedro Castaño 1

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.checat.2023.100666](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.checat.2023.100666 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2667109323001938&orderBeanReset=true)

Under an Elsevier [user license](http://www.elsevier.com/open-access/userlicense/1.0/)

Open archive

The bigger picture
------------------

_Operando_ methods are the quintessence of information-driven catalysis measurements to make structure-function correlations under working conditions. As the catalytic structure and function involve phenomena at different time and length scales, there is no single method that satisfies all. The design of the systems used in _operando_ measurements, where the reaction occurs, is paramount in making the measurements accurate and realistic. Here, we assess the leading efforts to expand the number of coupled techniques and obtain simultaneous information about the catalyst, adsorbed-trapped species, and products. At the same time, we assess the effort to make better designs and improve the data efficiency and accuracy, exploring the challenges, possibilities, and design guidelines. Our work proclaims the necessity of a multidisciplinary collaboration to design and interpret the information obtained in these instruments rationally, from physical chemistry to chemical engineering.

Summary
-------

The _[operando](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/operando "Learn more about operando from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ methodology is instrumental in catalysis science to assess catalyst structure, performance, dynamics, and kinetics under working conditions. This review analyzes the progress achieved mainly in thermal catalysis to combine different techniques and obtain the interdependency between catalyst structure, function, and reaction media. We analyze various materials of construction, reactor designs, contact types of the catalyst with the reactant, and modes of operation. We also highlight recent studies on combining these techniques and augmenting the obtained data by focusing on instrumentation and experimental design. We review the different reactors/cells used for different applications to understand the type of information received, limitations, and the design principles of these instruments. We provide our viewpoints on integrating spectroscopy, catalysis science, and reaction engineering; these advanced _operando_ techniques can offer a more comprehensive image of catalysts at work.

Introduction
------------

Our basic understanding of catalysis has profited from the simultaneous analysis of active sites and adsorbed species under reaction conditions (_[operando](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/operando "Learn more about operando from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_; [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)A), which can be extended to all facets of catalysis (i.e., homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis among others). Many studies have reported changes in reaction mechanisms or catalytic features in different chemistries upon the change of catalysts or operating conditions using these approaches in specifically designed cell reactors. Despite decades of research on this subject, there is considerable room for improvement in designing and developing various type of (cell) reactors ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)B) to obtain relevant kinetic information from catalysts at work. Over the last 10 years, rapid progress has been achieved in _operando_ spectroscopy ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)C) with >100 studies published in each spectroscopic domain (infrared [IR], Raman, and X-ray absorption spectroscopy [XAS] in particular; [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)D).

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2667109323001938-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (550KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2667109323001938-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (550KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2667109323001938-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Figure 1. The concept of _operando_ methodology and its evolution

(A) Types of methods performed in the _operando_ methodology.

(B) Types of cells/reactors to perform _operando_ spectroscopic experiments.

(C) Evolution of _operando_ spectroscopy publications from 2002 to 2022.

(D) Number of publications in _operando_ spectroscopy based on the techniques used. Sources: Scopus and Web of Science search using “_operando_ spectroscopy” in the article title field.

Multiple cells/reactors have been used, and new designs that combine two or more techniques have been proposed to provide a comprehensive image of a catalyst ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)B). Although these techniques have their own advantages and limitations, combining them in one experimental setup allows us to exploit their complementarities.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib1) This multi-technique approach not only provides the changes of dynamic processes but also (quasi-) simultaneous data acquisition, ensuring accurate structure-function correlations. Consequently, the structural information obtained goes beyond that of individual experimental methods. The success of the multi-technique approach depends on the rigorous optimization of experimental details. Therefore, bringing all the information together allows us to reveal a catalytic process under reaction conditions while examining the catalyst using different characterization techniques.

The intrinsic limitations of each analytical technique impose certain limitations on operating conditions, geometry, and reactor materials.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib2) Modeling approaches have been adopted to construct various _in situ_ and _operando_ cells/reactors. In the best case, an intrinsic kinetic model is developed in a reactor under negligible mass/heat transfer and contact between the reaction medium and catalyst (other effects; [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)A). Another approach directly involves the development of an _operando_ cell with proper reactor engineering considerations. Therefore, understanding these _operando_ cells/reactors at different scales is important because of the necessity of intrinsic data to form accurate structure-function correlations and scale-up procedures.

Despite a few studies discussing the multi-technique _operando_ approach, kinetic validation, and modeling strategies, there is rapid progress recently in this field using the multi-technique _operando_ approach. Furthermore, it is essential to differentiate and understand cells/reactors using a different-scale reaction engineering approach. Therefore, in this review, we present state-of-the-art _operando_ reactor technologies, including but not limited to combining two or more techniques with a main focus on heterogeneous catalysis (thermal). We start with basic definitions of the _in situ_/_operando_ techniques, then list out and differentiate various materials of construction used in the different reactors design based on available commercial/custom-made cells (cell reactors) and laboratory-type (microstructured, lab-scale, and pilot-scale) reactors. We systematically present the reported _operando_ approaches based on the type of contact (catalyst/reactant), mode of operation (continuous vs. transient), and different types of reactors ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#fig1)B), in addition to their limitations. We highlight recent developments that involve the multi-technique _operando_ approach (coupling multiple approaches in the same reactor or combining different techniques) in different facets of catalysis. The transport phenomena hinder gathering intrinsic kinetic data on the bed-catalyst level and are also holistically discussed. A distinction is made between heat/mass transfer effects arising from the measuring beam, reactor design, and sample preparation methods. Moreover, we grouped simulation-related studies based on nonreactive hydrodynamics, mass transport, and reaction kinetics. These are presented in terms of the different techniques to unveil the reaction mechanisms of catalytic processes in _operando_ reactors because of hydrodynamics, reactor configurations, and transport limitations. The catalytic spectroscopic cells mainly used in heterogeneous thermal catalysis should be intrinsic kinetic reactors based on fluid dynamic performance, experimental kinetic data, and external and internal mass transfer limitations. Finally, we propose a set of guidelines to rationally design these systems by bridging catalysis and reaction engineering concepts.

### Terminology

Catalysis scientists in the 1960s first used the term _in situ_, and _in situ_ spectroscopy became popular in the 1980s.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib3) The term _operando_ was coined by Eric Gaigneaux, Miguel A. Bañares, Gerhard Mestl, and Bert M. Weckhuysen at the 220 th ACS National Meeting in 2000 and first appeared in the catalytic literature in 2002 along with several publications placing the idea of _operando_ into practice.[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib4)

_Operando_ (working or operating) methodology is the simultaneous analysis of the catalyst activity and selectivity (function) reaction mixture using any analytical technique and the catalyst (structure) using characterization technique(s).[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib5),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib6) That is, _operando_ methodology is one step ahead of _in situ_ methodology, which means in place or on site and is applied to both working and non-working conditions while focusing only on the catalyst characterization part. The term _in situ_ is also used for spectra collection of a catalytic sample during treatment at variable temperature, pressure, and reactant concentration but without simultaneous product composition analysis, even when the treatment was performed under reaction conditions.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib6) Thus, with _operando_ methodology, we can understand the catalyst structure plus kinetics in one experiment.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib5),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib6) The data obtained in these systems enable us to assess structure-reactivity relationships more reliably (i.e., to correlate the catalyst structure and active site configuration with a specific catalytic performance).[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib2)

### Materials of construction

[Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#tbl1) summarizes the maximum operating conditions (temperature and pressure) for the most common _in situ_ and _operando_ techniques used to date, including _operando_ reactors that simulate laboratory conditions. The cell construction material limits these conditions, the spectroscopic method used, and the catalytic process studied, as certain conditions are inhospitable for the right analysis. Furthermore, the _operando_ technique puts boundary conditions on the construction material for certain parts of the cell, which emit and absorb the radiation and form the most delicate parts of the instrument. Unlike other reported reviews, we present these materials based on available commercial/custom-made cells (cell reactors) and laboratory-type (microstructured, lab-scale, and pilot-scale) reactors.

Table 1. Existing _in situ_/_operando_ techniques in catalysis with maximum operating conditions in all modes

| Technique | Window/capillary material | Maximum reported working conditions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Temperature (°C) | Pressure (bar) |
| IR | KBr/ZnSe/MgF 2/CaF 2/silicon/diamond/germanium/KRS-5 | 850[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib7) | 200[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib8) |
| XAS | quartzglass/beryllium/sapphire/polyimide/carbon/aluminum | 850[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib9) | 250[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib10) |
| Raman | quartz/sapphire/alumina | 800[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib11) | 200[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib12) |
| UV-vis | quartz/sapphire | 800[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib13) | 300[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib14) |
| EPR | quartz | 550[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib15) | 20[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib16) |
| XRD | sapphire/quartz | 1,100[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib17) | 35[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib17) |
| NMR | zirconia/glass | 250[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib18) | 400[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib18) |

#### Cell reactors

In this category (mainly referring to the commercial cells), optical-grade quartz windows are used in many _operando_ techniques (hard XAS, Raman, and UV-visible [UV-vis]) because of ease of operation, low brittleness, and established workshops for machining, high thermal resistance, and catalytic inertness. Other techniques, such as soft X-ray (energy,<5 keV; probing depth, 1–500 nm) and IR spectroscopies, require dedicated spectroscopic windows. Multiple construction materials are used for _operando_ X-ray studies (XAS and X-ray diffraction [XRD]) depending on the temperature, pressure, and specific reaction conditions. Quartz is the most applied window material for XAS studies (hard XAS: energy, >5 keV; probing depth, >500 nm), while sapphire is used in XRD measurements when subject to harsh conditions. Borosilicate, [polyimide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/polyimide "Learn more about polyimide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and carbon windows (used in both soft and hard XAS) are applied at low/ambient pressure and low temperature (<550°C). Quartz, Kapton, sapphire, and [nitrides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nitride "Learn more about nitrides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of boron (BN) and aluminum (AlN), which could be used in both soft-XAS (mainly) and hard-XAS measurements, are preferred for high-pressure and high-temperature _operando_ XAS investigations.

For _operando_ IR cells: probing depth varies depending on the mode of operation, 50 nm to 1.5 μm for diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and attenuated total reflectance (ATR) Fourier transform IR (FTIR). Quartz strongly absorbs in the near-IR region and IR-transmitting materials are required to aid sampling, as listed in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#tbl1). In practical applications, KBr, CaF 2, and ZnSe are popular for _operando_ transmission/diffuse reflectance IR experiments. For ATR-FTIR _operando_ measurements, diamond, germanium, silicon, ZnSe, and thallium bromoiodide (KRS-5) are popular owing to their high refractive indices and compatibility with aqueous media. For high-pressure and moderate-temperature _operando_ measurements, CaF 2 and ZnSe have been widely used in various investigations. During _operando_ IR measurements involving stainless steel as the material of construction (e.g., DRIFTS), it is essential to consider reactive impurities that can affect spectral analysis (e.g., the _in situ_ formation of Ni(CO)4 on active metals) and the catalytic process.[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib19) When combining XAS in IR cells, CaF 2 and MgF 2 windows can be used owing to their high chemical and thermal stability. However, the crystallinity of these windows can interfere with XAS measurements.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib20) Part of these windows can be laser drilled in an XAS beam path and filled with carbon-based glue to combine XAS and IR spectroscopy to overcome this issue.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib20)

For _operando_ Raman and UV-vis spectroscopic (probing depth varies depending on the laser, typically a few hundred nanometers to a few micrometers) cells, quartz (optical grade), sapphire, and alumina windows are used. In this context, it is essential to use high-quality spectroscopic window materials because of the intrinsically low S:N ratio of the spectra collected under _operando_ conditions.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib21) For _operando_ NMR (liquid and solid-state) measurements, glass NMR tubes (under mild conditions) and zirconia-based rotors (for high pressure and temperatures) were used.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib18),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib22) Moreover, window and reactor materials must have good thermal and mechanical stability, mainly when operating under harsh reaction conditions, depending on the catalytic process and spectroscopic technique.

#### Capillary/microstructured reactors

Quartz reactors were used in _operando_ electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR)/Raman/UV-vis/XAS methods. For _operando_ XAS, depending on the X-ray energy and reaction conditions, the reactor wall thickness affects the measurements that allow the passage of radiation through the catalytic material.[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib23) The use of beryllium (for _operando_ XAS), reported by Bare et al.,[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib24) exhibited high transmission (capillary reactor with a thickness range of 0.5–0.75 mm) at high temperatures and pressures. Borosilicate or polyimide tubing used for XAS (both soft and hard rays could be used) is cheaper, easier to work with,and presents high-quality data acquisition. However, these reactors have significant limitations. For example, they are fragile and deform at temperatures below 400°C.

Moreover, they tend to crack easily even when [polytetrafluoroethylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/polytetrafluoroethylene "Learn more about polytetrafluoroethylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PTFE) ferrules are used and have a limited pressure rating of a few atmospheres. However, glassy carbon (vitreous carbon) is suitable for high-temperature, high-pressure experiments with good X-ray transmittance. It is commercially available cheaply and can be machined into required shapes and sizes. For _operando_ measurements in the transmission mode (e.g., XAS), tubes with a lower wall thickness and a larger inner (or an optimized) diameter facilitate the radiation transmission.[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib25) The wall thickness and diameter effects have been analyzed to measure the XAS spectra and kinetics.[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib26) However, the transmission of X-rays depends on not only cell thickness but also the optical properties of samples.

#### Laboratory/pilot-scale reactors

In this category, quartz (high-purity) reactors are primarily used for XAS (soft/hard-XAS) measurements, and coupling XAS with UV-vis and/or Raman techniques with fiber optics is performed in the same reactor.[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib23),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib27) In addition to capillary reactors, Be is used in the laboratory-scale _operando_ XAS reactor design, which allows measurements at temperatures up to 600°C and 14 bar.[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib28) Despite the XAS data acquisition possible below 5 keV (soft X-ray absorption because of the wall collection; low X-ray absorption of Be reactor/wall), the Be reactor is expensive and difficult to operate because it may present health risks if improperly handled. A pure-grade Al reactor measured synchronous, spatially resolved energy-dispersive XAS and DRIFTS (with CaF 2 windows).[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib29) To ensure good transmission signals were obtained via sample and cell reactors, the reactor walls on both sides were thinned to 250 μm by drilling a trench having a length of 50 mm along the sample channel. For larger reactor volumes, two or three probes were used to examine the material along different positions of the catalytic bed. In this case, specially designed probes (Raman or UV-vis) were used with different lenses and windows (e.g., sapphire for Raman) suitable for specific applications.[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323001938#bib30)

In summary, depending on the specific application and spectroscopic technique, different materials of constructions are available for an _operando_ reactor/cell design that could withstand temperatures up to −194°C to 1,000°C and pressures as high as 100 bar. The reactor designs are possible at different scales (microstructured to pilot scales) that allow the coupling of up to four spectroscopic techniques. A detailed overview on the implementation of various materials of construction used in different types of _operando_ reactors at different scales is provided next.

Classification of _operando_ reactors
-------------------------------------

It is not straightforward to identify a univocal classification for _[operando](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/c

Chapter 7 Catalytic reaction engineering
========================================

Author links open overlay panel

Show more

[https://doi.org/10.1016/S0167-2991(08)63811-2](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0167-2991(08)63811-2 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0167299108638112&orderBeanReset=true)

Publisher Summary
-----------------

Catalytic reaction engineering is a scientific discipline which bridges the gap between the fundamentals of catalysis and its industrial application. Starting from insight into reaction mechanisms provided by catalytic chemists and surface scientists, the rate equations are developed, which allow a quantitative description of the effects of the reaction conditions on reaction rates and on selectivities for desired products. The study of intrinsic reaction kinetics, that is, those determined solely by chemical events, belongs to the core of catalytic reaction engineering. Very close to it lays the study of the interaction between physical transport and chemical reaction. Such interactions can have pronounced effects on the rates and selectivities obtained in industrial reactors. They have to be accounted for explicitly when scaling up from laboratory to industrial dimensions. The major reactor categories discussed in the chapter includes: batch reactor, plug flow reactor, continuous flow stirred tank reactor (CSTR), and nonisothermal reactors. The chapter presents an analysis of the effect of the finite rate at which reaction components and/or heat are supplied to or removed from the locus of reaction, that is, the catalytic site.

*   ### [Kinetics of cinnamaldehyde hydrogenation-concentration dependent selectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0920586100006349)

2001, Catalysis Today  Show abstract For the isothermal hydrogenation of cinnamaldehyde over Pt/C/monolithic catalysts three kinetic models have been evaluated, each model based on different sets of assumptions. A single-site and a two-site model with the surface reactions as rate-controlling steps were unable to describe the experimental data satisfactorily, while a single-site model with adsorption, surface reactions, and desorption all rate controlling could describe the experimental data adequately. This model includes apparent direct reaction steps from reactant to various end-products, due to the competition between desorption and subsequent conversion of intermediates at the catalyst surface. The selectivity to the unsaturated alcohol increases with increasing reactant concentration, which is ascribed to a change in adsorption mode, possibly due to some assembling phenomena of the reactant at the catalyst surface.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilize advanced tools such as spectroscopy and chromatography for precise kinetic measurements, allowing real-time assessment of catalytic reaction dynamics and product formation.
Implement sophisticated data acquisition systems that integrate multiple measurement techniques, ensuring comprehensive data collection on catalytic performance and reaction conditions.
Establish rigorous safety protocols, including risk assessments and emergency procedures, to protect personnel and equipment during high-pressure and high-temperature catalytic reactions.
